book_claims.claim: Regular legume consumption (3+ servings/week) associated with longest lifespan among Loma Linda Adventists
book_claims.mechanism: High fiber, plant protein, and complex carbohydrates; low glycemic load; does not activate pro-aging pathways like animal protein

book_claims.claim: Legumes are a core food in all centenarian blue-zone diets (Okinawa, Sardinia, Calabria, Loma Linda, Costa Rica, Greece)
book_claims.mechanism: Plant protein without IGF-1-elevating effect of animal protein; high fiber for gut health

book_claims.claim: Beans eaten almost every day by Molochio centenarians; legumes are primary protein source in Longevity Diet
book_claims.mechanism: High plant protein, fiber, and complex carbohydrates without animal-protein IGF-1 effects

book_claims.claim: Legumes associated with healthy aging, protection from cardiovascular disease, and cancer prevention
book_claims.mechanism: High in plant-based protein, complex carbohydrates, fiber, vitamins, and minerals; low in saturated fat; cause a lower insulin release than processed carbohydrates

book_claims.claim: Intake of beans reduces the risk of heart disease in women
book_claims.mechanism: Plant protein and soluble fiber lower LDL cholesterol and reduce cardiovascular risk

supplementary_research.finding: Pea protein supplementation significantly reduced systolic and diastolic blood pressure compared to dairy protein in a randomized controlled trial (PMID 19939654)
Regular legume consumption including peas is inversely associated with type 2 diabetes risk; peas have a glycemic index of ~22, among the lowest of starchy foods (PMID 22916807)

Peas provide 5 g protein and 4.5 g fiber per 100 g cooked alongside folate, vitamin K, and manganese; their soluble fiber content feeds beneficial bifidobacteria, supporting microbiome-mediated longevity pathways

texture: soft and creamy when cooked through

culinary_pairings.tradition: Italian (Longevity Diet recipe: rice with zucchini and peas)

nutrient_highlights.bioavailability_notes: Plant protein; lower leucine content than animal sources reduces IGF-1 and mTOR activation
Mix of soluble and insoluble fiber; soluble portion fermented into propionate and butyrate by gut bacteria
Water-soluble; critical for one-carbon metabolism and DNA methylation
Fat-soluble; absorption enhanced with dietary fat; important for vascular calcification prevention

synergies.description: Rice and peas together form a complete amino acid profile; this is the basis of the Longevity Diet 'riso con zucchine e piselli' dish
Fat from olive oil enhances vitamin K bioavailability from peas and slows carbohydrate absorption
Small amounts of umami-rich anchovies amplify the savory flavor of peas while adding EPA/DHA omega-3s to the dish
